---
title: "Coffee Quality Analysis"
subtitle: "Multivariate Structure of Professional Cupping Scores"
author: "Allyson Lopez, Xinyu Zhang, Arnav Kanekar"
date: "2026-06-03"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    fig-align: center
    embed-resources: true
    center : true
editor: source
---

# Introduction

## Motivation

- Specialty coffee is scored by certified Q-graders on six sensory attributes:
  **aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity, body, balance**.
- These expert scores directly affect a coffee's price and market value.
- We use multivariate methods to understand the structure behind the scores and
  how coffees relate to one another.

## Research Questions

| | Question | Methods |
|---|----------|---------|
| **RQ1** | What latent dimensions underlie cupping scores? | PCA, factor analysis |
| **RQ2** | Do coffee origins differ in their sensory profiles? | MANOVA, LDA |
| **RQ3** | Do clusters match known categories? | cluster analysis |


# Data

## The Data
  
- **Source:** Coffee Quality Institute (CQI), via the public *Coffee Quality
  Database*.
- **Observations:** Arabica coffee samples evaluated by professional cuppers.
- **Main variables:** six sensory attributes scored on a 0–10 scale:
  aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity, body, and balance.
- **Metadata:** country of origin, region, variety, processing method, and altitude.

## Data Cleaning

- Removed one corrupt record and rows with missing sensory scores.
- Kept countries with at least 30 samples to avoid extremely small origin groups.
- Removed uniformity, clean cup, and sweetness because they were almost always 10
  and contributed little variation.

Result: **1,099 Arabica coffees across 11 countries**, analyzed using six
sensory attributes.

# Exploratory Analysis

## Sample Sizes and Scores

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/01_country_counts.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/02_score_dist.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

- Sample sizes are imbalanced (Mexico 236, Thailand 32).
- Total Cup Points are centered around 82, with most coffees between 80–85 and a small left tail of lower-scoring samples.

## Correlation Structure

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figures/03_corr_heatmap.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
- All six attributes are positively correlated (r between 0.54 and 0.85).
- A coffee scoring well on one attribute tends to score well on the others.
- This shared variation motivates **principal component analysis**.
:::
::::

# RQ1: Principal Component Analysis

## Variance Explained: One Dominant Quality Dimension

PCA was applied to the six standardized sensory attributes.

[The data were appropriate for dimension reduction: KMO = 0.906 and Bartlett's test p < 0.001.]{style="font-size:0.68em; color:#5f5f5f;"}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
| PC | $\lambda$ | % Var | Cum % |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| PC1 | 4.36 | **72.6** | 72.6 |
| PC2 | 0.49 | 8.1 | 80.8 |
| PC3 | 0.39 | 6.6 | 87.3 |
| PC4 | 0.36 | 6.1 | 93.4 |

<br>

- PC1 explains **72.6%** of the total variation.
- Only PC1 has an eigenvalue greater than 1.
- This suggests that the six sensory attributes are largely driven by one shared
  dimension: **overall coffee quality**.
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
![](figures/05_pca_scree.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

## PCA Biplot

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
![](figures/06_pca_biplot.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
- **PC1 = overall quality (horizontal):**  
  all six arrows point right, so moving right means higher sensory scores across the board.  
  The color gradient also shows that higher PC1 is associated with higher Total Cup Points.

- **PC2 = weak style contrast (vertical):**  
  body and balance point upward, while aroma, flavor, and aftertaste point downward.  
  This mainly separates fuller-bodied coffees from more aroma-forward coffees.
:::
::::

## Factor Analysis: Same Story as PCA

Factor analysis gives a similar conclusion to PCA: the six sensory scores are
mostly driven by one common quality dimension.

- A **one-factor model** explains about **67%** of the variance.
- A **two-factor varimax solution** suggests a weak secondary split.

| Attribute | Factor 1 | Factor 2 |
|---|:--:|:--:|
| aroma | **0.61** | 0.45 |
| flavor | **0.87** | 0.45 |
| aftertaste | **0.67** | 0.61 |
| acidity | 0.57 | 0.52 |
| body | 0.40 | **0.67** |
| balance | 0.45 | **0.72** |

Factor 1 is more related to **flavor, aftertaste, and aroma**, while Factor 2 is
more related to **body and balance**. Since several attributes load on both
factors, the secondary split is weak.

# RQ2: Origin differences



# RQ3: Cluster Analysis

## Do coffees naturally form groups?

Goal:

- Determine whether coffees form natural sensory clusters.
- Compare clusters to known categories such as country of origin.

Methods:

- K-means clustering
- Hierarchical clustering
- Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)

Data:

- Six standardized sensory attributes: aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity,
  body, balance.

## Choosing the Number of Clusters

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/elbow_plot.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/silhouette_plot.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

- The elbow plot suggested approximately 2–3 clusters.
- The silhouette score was highest at k = 2.
- We retained k = 3 to allow more detailed subgroup interpretation.


## K-Means Cluster Visualization

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/cluster_plot.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
- Clusters showed moderate separation in the reduced PCA space.
- Some overlap existed between groups, suggesting that coffee sensory profiles vary continuously rather than forming completely distinct categories.
- The first two dimensions explained about 69% of total variation.
:::
::::

## Hierarchical Clustering and Cluster Agreement

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figures/dendrogram_plot.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

### Adjusted Rand Index

| Comparison | ARI |
|---|---:|
| Clusters vs Country | 0.046 |

- The dendrogram supported the presence of multiple sensory groupings.
- However, agreement between clusters and country labels was weak.
- Coffees from different origins often shared similar sensory profiles.

:::
::::

# Conclusions

## Conclusions

- The sensory data is well-suited to dimension reduction (KMO = 0.906;
  Bartlett $p < 0.001$).
- Coffee sensory quality is **essentially one-dimensional**: PC1 captures ~73%
  of the variation, with all six attributes loading strongly and positively.
- A minor secondary axis (~8%) reflects **style** (aromatic vs full-bodied),
  not overall quality.

## RQ3 Conclusions

- Coffee sensory profiles showed evidence of natural groupings.
- Approximately 2–3 clusters appeared reasonable.
- Clusters were only weakly associated with country of origin.
- This suggests that flavor characteristics overlap substantially across regions.
- Sensory quality may vary more continuously than discrete geographic categories imply.

## Limitations

- Imbalanced sample sizes across countries.
- Selection bias: CQI-submitted coffees skew high quality (scores cluster
  80–85).
- Halo effect: a grader's overall impression may bleed into every sub-score.
- Arabica only; Robusta excluded (28 samples).


## References

- Coffee Quality Institute, *Coffee Quality Database* (jldbc mirror).
- Project code: `github.com/allysonlopez/Stats140-240-Final-Project`.

## Thank you. 
**Questions?**
